# Mnemosyne for VS Code

Browse, search, and manage Mnemosyne memory directly in VS Code.

## Features

- **Memory sidebar** — View recent memories without leaving your editor
- **Search** — Full-text search across all stored memories

- **Stats** — See memory counts per tier at a glance
- **Auto-refresh** — Memories update on file save (configurable)

structure.rawContent:
# Mnemosyne for VS Code

Browse, search, and manage Mnemosyne memory directly in VS Code.

## Features

- **Memory sidebar** — View recent memories without leaving your editor
- **Search** — Full-text search across all stored memories
- **Store** — Save important context to Mnemosyne with a command
- **Stats** — See memory counts per tier at a glance
- **Auto-refresh** — Memories update on file save (configurable)

## Requirements

- [Mnemosyne](https://github.com/AxDSan/mnemosyne) installed (`pip install mnemosyne-memory`)
- Python 3.9+ available on PATH

## Extension Settings

| Setting | Default | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| `mnemosyne.dbPath` | `~/.hermes/mnemosyne/data/default.db` | Path to the Mnemosyne database |
| `mnemosyne.autoRefresh` | `true` | Refresh memories on file save |

## Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `Mnemosyne: Refresh Memories` | Refresh the memory list |
| `Mnemosyne: Search Memories` | Search across all memories |
| `Mnemosyne: Store a Memory` | Save a new memory |
| `Mnemosyne: Open Mnemosyne MCP Config` | Open the MCP config file |

## For Developers

```bash
git clone https://github.com/AxDSan/mnemosyne.git
cd integrations/vscode-mnemosyne
npm install
npm run compile
```

Then press F5 in VS Code to launch the extension host.

tsconfig.json.description: TypeScript compiler configuration for the extension

README.md.relationship: README documents usage and developer setup for the VS Code extension whose metadata is in package.json and build config in tsconfig.json
README.md.shared_context: VS Code Mnemosyne extension - documentation, manifest, and build config

package.json.relationship: package.json defines the extension metadata, commands, views, and settings documented in README.md; tsconfig.json compiles the TypeScript source referenced by package.json's main entry
package.json.shared_context: Extension manifest defining commands, views, settings, and build toolchain

tsconfig.json.relationship: tsconfig.json compiles TypeScript source to JS output (./out/extension.js) as specified in package.json's main and scripts; README documents the compile step for developers
tsconfig.json.shared_context: TypeScript build configuration for the VS Code extension

README.md.description: README documentation for the VS Code Mnemosyne extension
README.md.purpose: Document the VS Code Mnemosyne Memory Browser extension: its features, requirements, settings, commands, and developer setup instructions
keyFindings: Extension provides five core features: memory sidebar, full-text search, memory storage command, tier-based stats view, and auto-refresh on file save
Requires Mnemosyne (pip install mnemosyne-memory) and Python 3.9+ on PATH
Two configurable settings: mnemosyne.dbPath (default: ~/.hermes/mnemosyne/data/default.db) and mnemosyne.autoRefresh (default: true)
Four commands exposed: Refresh Memories, Search Memories, Store a Memory, Open Mnemosyne MCP Config
Developer setup follows standard VS Code extension workflow: git clone, npm install, npm run compile, then F5 to launch
securityConcerns: Default dbPath points to ~/.hermes/mnemosyne/data/default.db - ensure this path is not exposed via screenshots/sharing
MCP config command opens configuration file which may contain API keys or connection secrets
recommendations: Add troubleshooting section for common issues (Python not found, database path errors)
Consider documenting the MCP config file format and contents
Add a section on privacy: the extension reads local SQLite database, no data is sent externally

package.json.description: VS Code extension manifest and configuration
package.json.purpose: Define the VS Code extension metadata, activation behavior, contribution points (views, commands, settings), and build toolchain dependencies
keyFindings: Extension named 'mnemosyne-browser' with display name 'Mnemosyne Memory Browser' v0.1.0, published by 'AxDSan' under MIT license
Repository lives at https://github.com/AxDSan/mnemosyne
Requires VS Code engine ^1.85.0
Activation on-demand via 'onView:mnemosyneMemories' - no startup performance impact
Contributes an activity bar view container ('Mnemosyne') with a memory.svg icon
Two webview views: 'mnemosyneMemories' (Memories) and 'mnemosyneStats' (Stats)
Four commands registered: refresh, search, remember, openMcpConfig
Three configuration properties: dbPath (string), mcpCommand (string, default 'mnemosyne mcp'), autoRefresh (boolean, default true)
Build system: TypeScript compilation via tsc, linting via eslint
Dev dependencies: @types/vscode, @types/node, typescript ^5.3.0, eslint ^8.50.0
securityConcerns: mnemosyne.mcpCommand setting runs an arbitrary command string - ensure proper input validation
Webview-based views could potentially be exploited if unsanitized content is rendered; standard VS Code webview isolation applies
recommendations: Add a marketplace category like 'Programming Languages' or 'Machine Learning' instead of 'Other' for better discoverability
Consider adding activationEvents for the openMcpConfig command so it works before view activation
Add 'mnemosyne' to the extension icon path check - verify assets/memory.svg exists in the packaged extension

tsconfig.json.description: TypeScript compiler configuration for the extension
tsconfig.json.purpose: Configure the TypeScript compiler to compile src/ TypeScript source files into out/ CommonJS JavaScript for the VS Code extension runtime
keyFindings: Targets ES2021 with CommonJS module output - appropriate for Node.js-based VS Code extension host
Source code in src/ directory, compiled output in out/ directory
Strict mode enabled: full type checking including strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, etc.
esModuleInterop enabled: allows default imports from CommonJS modules with CJS/ESM interop
skipLibCheck enabled: skips type checking of declaration files for faster compilation
sourceMap enabled: generates .js.map files for debugging TypeScript in VS Code extension host
Excludes node_modules and out/ from compilation
securityConcerns: sourceMap enabled in production builds could expose source code to extension consumers; acceptable for VS Code extensions as source maps are not published to marketplace without sources
recommendations: Consider adding 'declaration': true if the extension exports types that other extensions might consume
Add 'forceConsistentCasingInFileNames': true for cross-platform filename consistency
Consider setting 'noUnusedLocals' and 'noUnusedParameters' for stricter code quality enforcement
